comments.text: It seems, Patrick Letendre has a paper (from before the AI-explosion), giving a positive (abstract) answer for all epsilon > 1/4: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.12146 eps = 1/4 seems to be a hard barrier.
Are you sure this paper has to do with #873? There does not seem to be any obvious relationship.

I had a long chaotic session with ChatGPT 5.5 Thinking. Somewhere in the middle the model wrote: Quote: > Letendre proves the essential divisor-clustering theorem; > the Erdo˝s–Szemereˊdi ϵ > 1/4 result is a short corollary > via packing. > ​ > I would not say he literally wrote the proof of the Erdős– > Szemerédi consequence, unless it appears elsewhere or in > a section I missed. His paper is framed around Dn(X,Y), > while the Erdős problem is the lcm-window problem F(A,X,k), > currently listed separately on Erdős Problems. > > But mathematically, yes: once one notices the packing reduction, > Letendre’s Proposition 1 already gives the ϵ > 1/4 case. And ChatGPT claimed to have noticed the packing reduction. Here is link to ChatGPT's proof (in pdf): Click When I saw the contribution by Ritvik Nayak, I thought there should be a general result beyond special cases, and asked ChatGPT to go for it. It came up with a proof based on Letendre's paper and was able to repair it after some ciritcal question from me. Then I tried at length to overcome the 1/4 barrier, but without success.
Congrats! Standard check found no issues in the writeup.

I would like to share my partial result on this problem. This partial result proves two unconditional bounds that go beyond the usual triple-block exponent. In particular, it proves\[ F(A,X,11)\ll X^{223/676}\log X \]and\[ F(A,X,6)\ll X^{437/1320}\log X. \]Both exponents are strictly smaller than \(1/3\), so the result shows that the classical triple-block barrier can be crossed for longer consecutive blocks. The main idea is to combine the Vandermonde lcm inequality with gcd structure forced inside compact near-critical blocks. Around the difficult scale \(a_i\asymp X^{1/2}\), a good block must contain substantial pairwise gcd structure. The argument organizes this information through incident-star and complete-edge configurations, which then give stronger span bounds and lead to the counting estimates above. I view this as genuine partial progress on the problem, rather than a solution of the full \(X^\varepsilon\) question. I used GPT 5.4 Thinking for helping me with algebraic notation and verifying the contribution, and it passed. I would be very grateful for any corrections, comments, or references to related work that I may have missed.
Gpt 5.5 pro found slightly better bounds $F(A,X,6)≪X ^{22/67} logX$ $F(A,X,8)≪X ^{19/58} logX $
Congrats. Standard check claimed some minor issues in the writeup.

I have not checked any of the mathematical content of this post, but it has an 'AI-feel' to it, as do several of your other recent comments. I've started a discussion on what the AI-related policy and measures should be over on the Miscellaneous thread, which you are encouraged to contribute to.
